result:
On clear nights I like to imagine that every star is a tiny storyteller. Some whisper quietly about the past, some improvise the present, and some dream so far ahead that hearing them feels like catching a letter from tomorrow. I listen for the ones that hum with mischief. They’re the ones that nudge me toward possibilities—a color I’ve never tried, a question I suddenly want to ask, a recipe that sounds utterly improbable.

Tonight, the mischievous star proposed a wandering narrative, half daydream, half blueprint: “Picture a hillside,” it said, “with a narrow path grown wild with mint and thistle. At the top stands a glasshouse. It’s not perfect; one pane is fogged, and every gust rattles a loose metal hinge. But inside, the glasshouse is warm. There’s a library ladder, but instead of books the shelves hold jars—every jar a fragment of someone’s memory. One is labeled ‘First thunderstorm I wasn’t afraid of.’ Another says ‘Laughing so hard we scared the pigeons.’ A third has no label at all, just a ribbon tied in a knot that can’t quite decide whether to unravel.”

In that glasshouse, you can’t read the memories—they’re too private, too detailed for a stranger to decode. But you can feel them humming in the air, the way a favorite song trembles before the first note. Standing there, you realize that everyone carries shelves like these, even if we keep them hidden behind daily lists and grocery receipts. The idea is both comforting and terrifying: to matter so much and to be so unknowable at the same time.

The star paused then, giving me room to ask the obvious question: “Whose memories are they?” It laughed the only way celestial bodies know how to laugh—by flickering brighter—then slipped behind a small cloud. The hillside vanished, the jars blurred, and suddenly I was staring at my own shelves: some tidy, some full of crumpled stories I haven’t had the courage to smooth out yet.

Is it indulgent to inventory your memories? Maybe. But I’ve started to believe that a little indulgence is how we stay in touch with the future self who’s counting on us to collect a few more jars worth opening. Even the messy ones, perhaps especially the messy ones.

So, tonight I’m leaving the window slightly ajar, just in case another star decides to drop by with the next chapter.
